content.full_text:
Fern PolrrrcAl Pnecrrces CoMMrssroN
-128 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacranrento. CA 95811-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
April 13,2009
Dave Whittum
Councilmember
City of Sunnyvale
456 West Olive Avenue
Sunnyvale, CA 94088-3707
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-09-075
Dear Mr. Whittum:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' Nothing in this letter should be
construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any
conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. In addition, this
letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the
Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby
(1975) I FPPC ops. 71.)
Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We
therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws
such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.
QUESTION
May you make, participate in making, or influence govemmental decisions
relating to the reauthorization of the Downtown Sunnyvale Business Improvement
District (the "BID") despite owning a home within 500 feet of the boundaries of the BID?
I Th. Politi.al Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 1000 rhrough 9 1014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
PoliticalPractices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the Califbrnia
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2. Division 6 of the Califbrnia Code of
Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.


File No. 4-09-075
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
Your economic interest in your home within 500 feet of the boundaries of the BID
is directly involved in governmental decisions to reauthorize the BID. The financial
effect of these decisions is presumed to be material. Accordingly, you may not make,
participate in making, or influence the decisions unless you can rebut the presumption, by
showing that it is not reasonably foreseeable the govemmental decision *1ll hur" ory
financial effect on your real property, and determine that there will be no reasonably
foreseeable material financial effects on any other economic interests you may have.
FACTS
You are a member of the City of Sunnyvale City Council. On March 24,2009,
the city council voted on a resolution to approve the BID Annual Report for the fiscal
year 200812009 and a Resolution of tntention to reauthorize the BID for the fiscal year
2OO9l2OlO atapublichearingonApril 29,2009. Pendingreceiptof Commissionadvice,
you recused yourself from the March 24,2009, city council vote in light of the fact that
you personally own a home within 500 feet of the Loundaries of the BID. Cunently, you
seek advice pertaining to your future participation in the public hearing on April 29,
2009, and other future decisions to reauthorize the BID.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest,' in a
govemmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's economic interesrs. (Section 87L}3;Regulation 1g700(a).) The
Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given gou"mmintal decision, which
we apply to your question. (Regulation 18700(bXl)-(8).)
Step One: Are you a "public official?',
The Act's conflict-of- interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(bXl).) A "public official" is "every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or rocar government agenc y . . ..,,
(Section 82048.) As a member of the Sunnyvale City Council, you are a public official
within the meaning of the Act.2
2 If a public official's office is listed in Section 87200 (*872}Ofilers" include members of a city
council) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she
must: ( I ) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type oleconomic interest
involved in the decision as well as details of the economic intereit, as discussed inRegulation


File No. 4-09-075
Page No. 3
Step Two: Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contrictual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the auihority of his or her
position and without significant intervening substantive review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the govenimental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or
appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency.
(Regulation 18702.3.) You are making, participating in making, or influenclrg a
govemmental decision when acting in your role as a city council member.
Step Three: What are your'.economic interests?r,
Of the economic interests recognized under the Act3, those interests implicated by
your account of the facts are the following:
Real Property -- A public official has an economic interest in real property in
which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section SltOg(U);
Regulation 18703.2.) Presumably, you have an investment interest of $2,000 or more in
your personally-owned home. Accordingly, you have an economic interest in this
property.
Personal Financial Effects -- A public official always has an economic interest in
his or her personal finances. In particular, a government decision has a personal
financial effect on a public official if the decision will result in the personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities of the official increasing or decreasing. lsection SZtO:;
Regulation 18703.5.) 4
18702.5(bX I )(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the
duration of the discussion and./or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in Regulation 1E702.5,
subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)
3 our analysis is limited to the economic interest you have identified.
a We point out that public officials always have an economic interest in their personal finances.
However, any financial effect the decision could have on your personally-owed home ii considered to be an
effect on your real property interest and would not be analyzed separately under the "personal financial
effects" rules. (Regulation 1 8705.5(a).) Accordingly, the personil frnancial effects .ul. do", nor appear to
apply to your circumstances and we will not discuss it further.


File No. A-09-075
Phge No.4
Step Four: Is your economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the decision?
Regulation 18704.2(a) states, in pertinent part, that real property in which a public
official has an economic interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if '1t1he
real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of that real property, is
located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the properry
which is the subject of the governmental decision
From the facts you have provided, your personally-owned home is within 500 feet
of the boundaries of the BID, which is the property subject to a decision to reauthorize
the BID for fiscal year 2OO912010.6 Accordingly, you.i"onomic interest in this properry
is directly involved in the government decision.
Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on your economic interest?
Materiality
Having identified the economic interest involved and determined that the interest
is directly involved in the decision at issue, it is necessary to identify the materiality
standard appropriate to your economic interest in your home.
Under Regulation 187O5.2(a)(1), any financial effect of a governmental decision,
even one penny, on real property directly involved in the govemmental decision is
presumed to be material. (Regularion 18705.2(aXl).)
Foreseeability
Once a public official has determined the materiality standards applicable to each
of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is-"reasonably
foreseeable" that the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation
18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it
must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I Fppc ops. l9g.)
s "If a public official's economic interest is not directly involved in a governmental decision, it is
indirectly involved." (Regulation I 87M(a).)
You have presented several governmental decisions relating to the BID for our analysis. The
first two of these decisions are the city council's decisions to approve itre StO Annual Report ior fiscal
year 2N812009 and to adopt a Resolution of Intention and schedules for a public hearing bn April 2l,
2009, to reauthorize the BID. However, these decisions occurred on March 24,2OOg,orO th. iommission
will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(bX8)(A) and (c)(4)(A).) Accordingly, we
must decline to issue advice relating to these decisions at this time. We proceed only with an analyiii of
your participation in the public hearing on April 21,2009, or other future decisions to reauthorize the BID.


File No. ,4'-09-075
Page No. 5
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision
is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (1n re Thorner, supra,
at 198.) Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providin g advice (In
re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 7l), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a
determination that must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public
official.
However, based upon the facts provided, you have indicated a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on your economic interest in your home. While ultimately
the determination of whether the financial effect is material must be left up to your
informed judgment, the financial effect on your economic interest in your home is
presumed to be material because the property is within 500 feet of the property subject to
the govemmental decision. Accordingly, you may not participate in thi governmental
decisions to reauthorize the BID unless you can rebut the presumption of-uteri alfty.7
Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
govemmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally.
(Section 87 103; Regulation 18707 (a).)
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to
take part in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of
interest under the "legally required participation" exception. This exciption applies only in
certain very specific circumstances where the government agency would be paralyzed fiom
acting. (Section 87101; Regulation 13708.)
However, you have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally,'
or the "legally required participation" exceptions are applicable to youicircrrirt*""r, ,o
we will not address them further.
i This conclusion applies only to your participation in the April 2l,2}Og,public hearing and
future decisions to reauthorize the BID. We express no opinion as to your participaiion in decisions
relating to discrete parcels within the BID. However, when determining whetherin economic interest in
property is directly involved in a governmental decision under the 5fi) ioot rule, the plain language of
Regulation 18704.2(a)(l) requires that the distance be measured from the boundaries of the pioperty that is
the subject of the governmental decision. Unless "inextricably interrelated" to other gor"rr..ntul
decisions, the subject property of a govemmental decision specific to a discrete pur."I i, the parcel itself.
Decisions are only "inextricably interrelated" when the result of one decision will effectivelydetermine,
atfirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision. (Regulation | 8709.) If you need furtirer assisrance
relating to your participation in a specific decision involving a discrete parcel within the BID, you should
seek addition advice providing all relevant facts pertaining to the decision.


File No. 4-09-075
Page No. 6
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division
BGLjgl
